explicit_text: My name is Minejiro Shibata, born in 1905 in Shimizu, Shizuoka. I came from a farming family that also owned a fishing boat, though farming was our main livelihood. I immigrated to the United States in 1919 at the age of eighteen, motivated by my brother who had settled here earlier. My life in America involved fishing, attending school in Los Angeles, and building a fishing boat with friends in 1924.
implicit_text: My name is Minejiro, born in 1905 in Shimizu, Shizuoka. I came from a farming family that also owned a fishing boat, though farming was our main livelihood. I immigrated to America in 1919 at the age of eighteen, motivated by my brother who had settled there earlier. My life in the new country involved fishing, attending school in Los Angeles, and building a fishing boat with friends in 1924.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Shimizu, a part of Shizuoka Prefecture in Japan. Our family were farmers there, and we also owned a boat for tuna fishing, though we weren’t professional fishermen. Life in Shizuoka during my childhood was tough for farmers and townspeople alike, especially during events like the rice riots. It was a rural place where people worked hard and lived simply.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in Shimizu, a part of a coastal prefecture in Japan. Our family were farmers there, and we also owned a boat for tuna fishing, though we weren’t professional fishermen. Life in that region during my childhood was tough for farmers and townspeople alike, especially during events like the rice riots. It was a rural place where people worked hard and lived simply.

explicit_text: I was born in 1905, which corresponds to Meiji 35 in the Japanese calendar. The Meiji period was a time of great change in Japan, though my childhood memories mostly focus on rural life and farming in Shizuoka. It was a period when Japan was modernizing, but many farmers like my family still lived a hard life. I remember the social conditions that led to events like the rice riots during that era.
implicit_text: I was born in 1905, which corresponds to the 35th year of a major era in Japanese history. That era was a time of great change in Japan, though my childhood memories mostly focus on rural life and farming in Shizuoka. It was a period when Japan was modernizing, but many farmers like my family still lived a hard life. I remember the social conditions that led to events like the rice riots during that era.

explicit_text: I recall the rice riots that happened around 1915 or so, when the price of rice rose sharply and poor townspeople took revenge on the rich by burning their rice stores. The riots were a hard time for many, though my family of farmers was somewhat insulated because we grew our own crops. The poor could only afford to buy small amounts of rice, which was a common hardship then. I even went to see the ruins of the fires, which were still smoking.
implicit_text: I recall the rice riots that happened around 1915 or so, when the price of rice rose sharply and poor townspeople took revenge on the rich by burning their rice stores. The poor could only afford to buy small amounts of rice, which was a common hardship then. I even went to see the ruins of the fires, which were still smoking.

explicit_text: My family followed Buddhism, specifically the Zen sect, though our village wasn’t very religious. We didn’t often attend temple sermons, and the priests were people we knew personally, so their teachings didn’t hold much sway. I only learned it was the Zen sect after coming to the United States. Over time, I became less enthusiastic about religion and did not continue practicing Buddhism actively.
implicit_text: My family followed a form of Buddhism, specifically a meditative sect, though our village wasn’t very religious. We didn’t often attend temple sermons, and the priests were people we knew personally, so their teachings didn’t hold much sway. I only learned the specific sect after coming to America. Over time, I became less enthusiastic about religion and did not continue practicing actively.

explicit_text: Our family religion was Buddhism of the Zen sect, though I didn’t realize it was Zen until after I came to America. In my village, people didn’t pay much attention to the temple or the sermons, partly because the priests were locals and young. The Zen teachings were part of our background, but I personally didn’t follow them closely after leaving Japan. Religion was not a big part of my life growing up or later.

explicit_text: I heard the term Christianity when I was in Japan, but I never attended church or had much exposure to it during my childhood. It wasn’t a part of my family’s life or village culture. After coming to the United States, I never listened to a minister or engaged with Christianity either. Religion in general was not something I pursued actively.

Religion in general was not something I pursued actively.

explicit_text: I came to the United States in May 1919 when I was eighteen, following my brother who had settled here earlier. I arrived via Hawaii and San Francisco, then traveled to Los Angeles. The U.S. seemed a beautiful country to me, though life was not always easy. I worked as a fisherman on Terminal Island, attended school in Hollywood, and experienced both kindness and discrimination from Caucasians.
implicit_text: I came to America in May 1919 when I was eighteen, following my brother who had settled there earlier. I arrived via Hawaii and San Francisco, then traveled to Los Angeles. The country seemed beautiful to me, though life was not always easy. I worked as a fisherman on Terminal Island, attended school in Hollywood, and experienced both kindness and discrimination from Caucasians.

explicit_text: My brother traveled through several cities including Venice before deciding to settle in the United States. He had left Japan years before me, seeking a better life and eventually choosing America as his home. Venice was one of the stops on his journey, though I did not visit there myself. His travels motivated me to come to the U.S.

His travels motivated me to come to the U.S.

explicit_text: Before settling in the United States, my brother spent time in London among other cities like Venice and New York. He moved around looking for opportunities, and London was part of that journey. His experiences abroad encouraged me to join him in America. I did not personally go to London, but it was significant in my brother’s path.

explicit_text: My brother lived in New York City for some time before deciding to stay permanently in the United States. He traveled through New York as part of his search for a better life after leaving Japan. His decision to settle in the U.S. inspired me to come here in 1919. I did not visit New York myself, but it was important in my brother’s story.

explicit_text: When I immigrated to the United States in 1919, the ship stopped overnight in Hawaii. I think the ship I came on was the Saibei Maru. We didn’t stay long there; the next day the ship sailed directly to San Francisco. Hawaii was a brief but memorable stop on my journey to America.
implicit_text: When I immigrated to America in 1919, the ship stopped overnight in a Pacific island territory. I think the ship I came on was the Saibei Maru. We didn’t stay long there; the next day the ship sailed directly to San Francisco. That island was a brief but memorable stop on my journey to America.

explicit_text: I believe I came to the United States on the Saibei Maru in 1919. The ship stopped overnight in Hawaii before continuing to San Francisco. The voyage took about fourteen or fifteen days, and conditions on board were tough, with lice and poor food. Despite that, I felt at peace coming to a new country.
implicit_text: I believe I came to America on the Saibei Maru in 1919. The ship stopped overnight in a Pacific island territory before continuing to San Francisco. The voyage took about fourteen or fifteen days, and conditions on board were tough, with lice and poor food. Despite that, I felt at peace coming to a new country.

explicit_text: I arrived in San Francisco in May 1919 after the ship stopped briefly in Hawaii. I stayed on Angel Island for three or four days to complete immigration procedures. The experience there was mixed; the food was not good, and the facilities were uncomfortable, but the treatment was neither harsh nor kind. From San Francisco, I took a train to Los Angeles to meet someone who greeted me warmly.
implicit_text: I arrived in San Francisco in May 1919 after the ship stopped briefly in Hawaii. I stayed on an immigration island for three or four days to complete procedures. The experience there was mixed; the food was not good, and the facilities were uncomfortable, but the treatment was neither harsh nor kind. From San Francisco, I took a train to Los Angeles to meet someone who greeted me warmly.

explicit_text: After arriving in San Francisco, I traveled by train to Los Angeles in 1919. I lived on Terminal Island, where many Japanese resided and worked in the fishing industry. I attended school in Hollywood for about six months but struggled with English. Los Angeles was where I started building my new life, working as a fisherman and schoolboy to support myself.
implicit_text: After arriving in San Francisco, I traveled by train to Los Angeles in 1919. I lived on Terminal Island, where many Japanese resided and worked in the fishing industry. I attended school in Hollywood for about six months but struggled with English. Los Angeles was where I started building my new life, working as a fisherman and schoolboy to support myself.

explicit_text: Upon arriving in San Francisco in 1919, I was held at Angel Island for three or four days to go through immigration. The wooden walls were covered with wire netting, and I saw Chinese immigrants’ delicate carvings on the walls. The Japanese food served was free but not good, while American food cost twenty-five cents. The bathroom facilities were uncomfortable, with many toilet bowls side by side without doors, which was hard for me to accept.
implicit_text: Upon arriving in San Francisco in 1919, I was held at an immigration station on an island for three or four days to go through processing. The wooden walls were covered with wire netting, and I saw Chinese immigrants’ delicate carvings on the walls. The Japanese food served was free but not good, while American food cost twenty-five cents. The bathroom facilities were uncomfortable, with many toilet bowls side by side without doors, which was hard for me to accept.

explicit_text: At Angel Island, I noticed that Chinese people had left delicate letters and carvings on the walls. Their presence was a visible part of the immigrant experience there. Although I was Japanese, I was impressed by the artistry of their inscriptions. The immigration process affected many Chinese and Japanese immigrants alike.

Although I was Japanese, I was impressed by the artistry of their inscriptions. The immigration process affected many Chinese and Japanese immigrants alike.

explicit_text: When I arrived in Los Angeles, I was taken to the Southern Pacific station at Sixth and Central Avenue, not the Union Station. The SP station was separate from the Union Station at that time. From there, someone came to meet me, which made me very happy after my long journey.
implicit_text: When I arrived in Los Angeles, I was taken to the Southern Pacific station at Sixth and Central Avenue, not the main union station. The SP station was separate from the union station at that time. From there, someone came to meet me, which made me very happy after my long journey.

explicit_text: After landing in San Francisco, I took a train operated by the Southern Pacific Transportation Company to Los Angeles. The train brought me to the SP station rather than the Union Station. This was my first experience traveling by train in the U.S. and it connected me to the Japanese community in Los Angeles.

explicit_text: I lived on Terminal Island after arriving in Los Angeles, where many Japanese immigrants worked in the fishing industry. The white workers only came during the daytime when there was a fish catch, so Japanese was the dominant language there. Life was simple, with wooden houses built by the canning company and a Japanese store nearby. I started fishing there in May 1919 and continued for several years.

Life was simple, with wooden houses built by the canning company and a Japanese store nearby. I started fishing there in May 1919 and continued for several years.

explicit_text: Before moving to Terminal Island, many Japanese people lived in Port La Sail around the Santa Fe area. They relocated to Terminal Island when the canning company built houses for employees. This migration happened about three or four years before I arrived. The move helped establish a strong Japanese community on Terminal Island.

Santa Fe was part of the early Japanese immigrant experience in the region.

explicit_text: Some of my friends graduated from the University of Southern California, but many overworked themselves by balancing studies and family responsibilities. Unfortunately, some died from disease due to the strain. I wanted to attend high school but stopped studying because I struggled with English. College was very difficult to pursue without strong health and support.

explicit_text: Bonito was not specifically mentioned in my story. However, as a fisherman, I was involved in tuna fishing, and bonito is a type of fish related to that industry. Fishing was a major part of my life after immigrating, especially on Terminal Island, where we worked hard to earn money during the fishing seasons.
